name: GenieWorkspace
summary: Load one GenieWorkspace, keep manager ownership explicit, and route each mutation through the manager that owns that asset surface.
why_it_matters: A mature .dat project is not one giant mutable blob. The workspace is the entrypoint, while unit_manager, graphic_manager, sound_manager, tech_manager, civ_manager, and effect_manager own different edit surfaces.
how_it_works: The project loads the base dat once in main/main.py, acquires managers once, applies high-level systems, and saves at the end. Cross-asset features should compose several managers inside one feature apply_ module instead of blurring ownership in the root entrypoint.
project_evidence: GoKu RPG Genie Code/main/main.py loads the workspace once, gets the managers once, then calls class, world, character, inventory, and skill-tree systems.
apply_items.py uses graphic_manager, unit_manager, civ_manager, effect_manager, and tech_manager together inside one feature workflow.
pitfalls: Do not repeatedly load and save the workspace in leaf modules.
Do not explain a feature as if every manager can mutate every asset type.

name: base_unit_id
display_name: Unit Creation From A Stable Base Asset
summary: New units in this project are usually created from a known base unit, then narrowed by mutating only the fields that matter for the feature.
why_it_matters: Copying from a stable base keeps hidden engine defaults intact while letting the project control identity, footprint, selection, and linkage fields explicitly.
how_it_works: The project uses ws.unit_manager.create(name=..., base_unit_id=...) to copy a known game unit, then mutates identity and gameplay fields. Pick Class copies TENT_C, while inventory items copy OUTPOST so the new unit inherits a safe starting shape before radius and selection fields are reset.
project_evidence: Character_General/Pick_Class/pick_class_unit.py creates Pick Class from BuildingInfo.TENT_C.ID.
Inventory_System/Items/unit.py creates item units from BuildingInfo.OUTPOST.ID and then clears radius and selection behavior.
pitfalls: Do not mutate the original base unit in place when the feature needs a distinct unit id.
Do not skip identity and footprint cleanup when the copied base unit carries unwanted defaults.

name: standing_sprite_id1
summary: standing_sprite_id1, standing_sprite_id2, and dead_unit_id are the core unit-side linkage fields used throughout the project to connect units to graphics and secondary units.
why_it_matters: Most visual dat features only work once the unit-side ids are wired explicitly. Creating the asset is not enough; the unit must point to the right graphic or dead-unit component.
how_it_works: The project creates graphics and units separately, then assigns graphic.id into standing_sprite_id1 or both standing_sprite_id1 and standing_sprite_id2. Multi-part features also assign a created secondary unit into dead_unit_id so the main unit points to a dead-state asset chain.
project_evidence: apply_pick_class.py links unit.standing_sprite_id1 = graphic.id.
apply_potions.py links potion graphics through standing_sprite_id1 and standing_sprite_id2.
Heavenly_Bastion/link_components.py links the dead unit graphic first, then assigns heavenly_bastion_unit.dead_unit_id = heavenly_bastion_dead_unit.id.
pitfalls: Do not create a custom graphic and forget the unit-side sprite link.
Do not set dead_unit_id before the secondary unit exists.

name: file_name
summary: Non-particle graphics in the project are created through graphic_manager.add_graphic and then configured with file_name, layer, transparent_selection, replay_delay, frame_count, animation_duration, sequence_type, and angle_count.
why_it_matters: These are the recurring sprite-side fields that make a new static or standard animated dat graphic behave correctly when attached to a unit.
how_it_works: The project uses add_graphic(name) to create the graphic row, then fills the core visual fields. Pick Class and Pick Difficulty graphics show the standard non-particle template: one file_name, one layer, one frame, and one angle for a static object marker.
project_evidence: Character_General/Pick_Class/pick_class_graphics.py sets file_name, layer, transparent_selection, replay_delay, frame_count, animation_duration, sequence_type, and angle_count.
Mana bar graphics use the same base field family before adding wrapper deltas.
pitfalls: Do not assume add_graphic(name) alone is enough for a working in-game graphic row.
Do not mix sprite configuration with unrelated unit or tech edits when teaching the workflow.

name: particle_effect_name
summary: Particle-style marker graphics are normal graphics with particle_effect_name and a slightly different animation profile, then linked to a lightweight unit.
why_it_matters: Quest markers and other world indicators often need a particle-driven visual identity rather than a plain sprite file alone.
how_it_works: The project creates a graphic row through add_graphic, assigns particle_effect_name, and then configures layer, transparency, frame_count, animation_duration, sequence_type, and angle_count. The graphic is then linked into a marker or quest unit through the normal standing sprite linkage.
project_evidence: Character_General/Quests/Quest_Grey/quest_grey_graphics.py sets particle_effect_name = 'question_mark_grey' and uses a particle-style animation profile.
pitfalls: Do not treat the particle graphic as the full gameplay object when the feature still needs a unit anchor.
Do not reuse non-particle timing defaults blindly on marker graphics.

name: add_delta
summary: The project uses wrapper graphics with add_delta to offset or compose a base graphic instead of duplicating the underlying sprite logic everywhere.
why_it_matters: This is the clean pattern for overlays like mana bars, where the visible asset should inherit a base graphic but render with a controlled offset.
how_it_works: The mana bar workflow creates a base graphic from the source SLX name, then creates a second wrapper graphic and calls add_delta(graphic_id=base_graphic.id, offset_x=0, offset_y=-50, display_angle=-1). The wrapper becomes the asset that gets referenced by gameplay systems.
project_evidence: Graphics_Making/Mana_Bar/apply_mana_bar.py builds wrapper graphics named hero_mana_bar_*_percent and adds a delta with offset_y = -50.
pitfalls: Do not overwrite the base graphic when the feature expects a reusable wrapper.
Do not hide the offset logic inside unrelated system orchestration.

name: add_resource
summary: New resource slots are added through civ_manager and apply to every civilization, not just one local feature row.
why_it_matters: Inventory-style systems and custom dat resources depend on understanding that civ resources are global column additions across all civilizations.
how_it_works: The item workflow allocates one new resource slot per item through civ_manager.add_resource(default_value=0.0). The underlying tooling notes that resources are global, so add/remove operations must happen for all civilizations together, while per-civ values are edited through the civ resource accessor.
project_evidence: Inventory_System/Items/resource.py exposes add_item_resource(civ_manager) -> civ_manager.add_resource(default_value=0.0).
items_resources_data.json records each generated item resource id.
The vendored CivManager and ResourceAccessor docs in the project note that resources are global across all civilizations.
pitfalls: Do not describe a custom resource slot as if it exists only for one civ.
Do not hardcode resource ids in many feature files when the project already saves a shared mapping.

name: modify_tech
summary: The project uses effect commands such as resource_modifier, modify_tech, and enable_disable_unit to turn resource slots and tech rows into actual gameplay unlocks.
why_it_matters: In Genie dat work, a tech or resource row does nothing useful until an effect command chain connects it to a behavior change.
how_it_works: The item system creates one effect per item resource and then composes a separate enable effect that modifies the item tech state and enables the unit. The effect layer is where the abstract resource or tech ids become concrete gameplay changes.
project_evidence: Inventory_System/Items/effect.py creates an effect and adds a resource_modifier command.
Inventory_System/Items/enable_techs.py adds modify_tech and enable_disable_unit commands to the enable effect.
pitfalls: Do not assume a created tech unlocks content without an effect command path.
Do not scatter command ids and target ids across unrelated modules.

name: add_research_location
display_name: Tech Definition And Research Location Fields
summary: A tech needs more than a name and effect_id. The project explicitly wires required techs, min_required_techs, type, icon, repeatable flags, zeroed cost slots, and research locations.
why_it_matters: This is the recurring dat surface for making a tech actually researchable from a specific unit or keeping it hidden until another enable path exposes it.
how_it_works: The project uses tech_manager.add_new(name=..., effect_id=...) and then fills the rest of the tech row explicitly. create_item_technology sets one required tech slot, min_required_techs, type, icon, repeatable, three cost slots, and then adds a research location for the owning unit. The enable technology uses a hidden location_unit_id = -1 until the effect chain exposes the item techs.
project_evidence: Inventory_System/Items/technology.py creates an item technology with effect_id, required tech, zero cost slots, and add_research_location.
Inventory_System/Items/enable_techs.py creates a hidden enabling technology with location_unit_id = -1 and no visible button.
pitfalls: Do not stop at tech_manager.add_new when the tech still lacks prerequisites, cost, or research-location wiring.
Do not confuse a hidden enable technology with a visible research entry.

name: items_resources_data
summary: The item system is a full data-driven dat pipeline: create graphic, create unit, allocate resource, create effect, create tech, enable the tech, then save the generated id mapping.
why_it_matters: This is the clearest real project proof that the dat side should be taught as a chain of linked systems, not isolated field edits.
how_it_works: apply_items.py loops over source art files, creates the graphic and unit, links the sprites, allocates a global resource slot, creates an effect for that resource, creates a technology for that effect, enables the technology through a shared effect, and writes items_resources_data.json so later systems can rebalance or query the generated ids without reopening code.
project_evidence: Inventory_System/Items/apply_items.py implements the full pipeline in one coordinator.
main/items_resources_data.json stores the generated item_name -> item_resource_id mapping for later balancing and retrieval.
pitfalls: Do not bury generated ids in transient console output when a shared JSON mapping is needed later.
Do not separate the pipeline explanation into unrelated docs without showing the full chain.

name: task
summary: Production and custom action features depend on task rows or train-location rows on the acting unit or producer, not only on the produced unit definition.
why_it_matters: Agents often focus on the target unit and forget the producer-side command path that actually makes the feature reachable in-game.
how_it_works: The repo already teaches this as a recipe surface: create or retrieve the actor, create or retrieve the target unit, then add the build task or train-location mapping on the producer side. In practice, this is the difference between a valid data row and an actually reachable production flow.
project_evidence: The genie recipe layer in this repo already includes add_build_task_to_hero and wire_train_location_and_task because the GoKu workflow repeatedly depends on producer-side wiring.
create_item_technology adds a research location onto the unit side, which is the tech analogue of production-path wiring.
pitfalls: Do not validate only the produced unit row when the producer-side task path is missing.
Do not mix production-path edits with unrelated balance changes in one file.

name: config/ids.py
summary: Global ids should live in a shared registry, balance mappings should live in data files, and the output dat path should come from config or the user instead of a hardcoded machine path.
why_it_matters: This keeps the dat project maintainable, makes balance passes cheap, and prevents agents from inventing local output paths when they generalize your project structure.
how_it_works: The project centralizes stable graphic ids in config/ids.py, writes generated item resource mappings to JSON, and saves the built dat plus a registry artifact at the end of main/main.py. General repo guidance should preserve that structure but ask the user where to write the final dat if the target project does not already define an output path.
project_evidence: config/ids.py stores shared graphics and world object ids.
main/main.py saves the modified dat and save_registry output.
main/items_resources_data.json is the explicit balance-facing mapping file.
pitfalls: Do not hardcode one developer PC mod folder as the default output answer.
Do not scatter shared ids and balance data across unrelated feature files.
